1: ta tekna, hypakouete tois goneusin hymōn en kyriō, touto gar estin dikaion — "children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right" (hypakouō: to obey, listen under, respond to as an authority — from hypo + akouō): the address to children directly (ta tekna) assumes their presence in the assembled community where the letter is read — they are full members of the household of God who can receive instruction. En kyriō (in the Lord): the Lord-reference qualifies the obedience (ultimate allegiance belongs to the Lord, not parents absolutely) and motivates it (obedience to parents is a practice of the Lord-shaped life). Touto gar estin dikaion (for this is right, just) — the appeal to natural-order rightness supplements the appeal to Torah.
2: tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera — Paul cites Exodus 20:12 (Deuteronomy 5:16) with the qualifying phrase hētis estin entolē prōtē en epangeliai (which is the first commandment with a promise): prōtē en epangeliai — "first in/with promise." This is a notable rabbinic-style classification: Paul identifies the fifth commandment as uniquely carrying an attached promise, distinguishing it within the Decalogue. The Decalogue's first four commandments concern God directly; the fifth begins the human-relations series and carries the specific land-promise that Paul applies eschatologically.
3: hina eu soi genētai kai esē makrochronios epi tēs gēs — "that it may go well with you and that you may live long in the land": Paul reproduces the Deuteronomy version of the promise (hina eu soi genētai kai makrochronios esē epi tēs gēs agathēs: Deuteronomy 5:16 LXX). The land-promise (epi tēs gēs, upon the earth/land) is neither spiritualized away nor confined to Israel's territory — the gē (earth/land) functions eschatologically as the renewed creation that the meek will inherit (Matthew 5:5; cf. Psalms 37:11). The promise grounds the command in covenantal consequence: honoring parents is embedded in a world that rewards covenant faithfulness.
4: hoi pateres, mē parorgizete ta tekna hymōn — "fathers, do not provoke your children to anger" (parorgizō: to irritate thoroughly, provoke to wrath — the para intensifies the orgizō): Colossians 3:21 uses the synonym erethizō (to irritate, stir up). The address to fathers specifically (not "parents" — pateres, masculine) targets the paterfamilias, the Roman family head who held legal authority (patria potestas) over children and whose harshness was the primary threat to children's formation. Alla ektrephete auta en paideia kai nouthesia kyriou — "but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord" (ektrephō: to nourish, bring up; paideia: formation, training, discipline; nouthesia: admonition, instruction-by-words).
5: hoi douloi, hypakouete tois kata sarka kyriois meta phobou kai tromou en haplotēti tēs kardias hymōn hōs tō Christō — "slaves, obey your earthly masters with fear and trembling, with a sincere heart, as to Christ" (kata sarka kyriois: fleshly masters, masters according to the flesh — the qualifier marks the relativity of the human lordship by distinguishing it from the kyrios-relationship to Christ): meta phobou kai tromou (with fear and trembling) is Paul's idiom for the serious attentiveness owed in a relationship of great consequence (cf. 1 Corinthians 2:3; 2 Corinthians 7:15; Philippians 2:12). En haplotēti tēs kardias (with singleness/sincerity of heart) — undivided inner orientation, contrasted with eye-service.
6: mē kat' ophthalmodoulian hōs anthrōpareskoi alla hōs douloi Christou poiountes to thelēma tou theou ek psychēs — "not with eye-service as people-pleasers, but as slaves of Christ doing the will of God from the soul" (ophthalmodouleia: eye-slavery, service performed only when watched — NT hapax, also Colossians 3:22; likely Paul's coinage): anthrōpareskoi (people-pleasers): another rare compound; the opposite of the single-minded God-pleasing that Paul commends. Hōs douloi Christou (as slaves of Christ): the deeper identity relativizes the human slavery — every believer-slave is already a slave of Christ, which reframes all human service as ultimately directed toward Christ.
9: kai hoi kyrioi, ta auta poieite pros autous, aniēntes tēn apeilēn — "and masters, do the same to them, giving up the threat" (aniēntes: releasing, letting go of — from aniēmi, to let go, release; same word used for releasing prisoners): the command to "do the same things" (ta auta poieite) toward slaves creates a reciprocity that the standard household code tradition did not: masters are to apply the same sincere-hearted service-orientation toward their slaves that they require of their slaves. Eidotes hoti kai autōn kai hymōn ho kyrios estin en ouranois kai prosōpolēmpsia ouk estin par' autō — the theological grounding: both slave and master have the same Lord in heaven, and this Lord shows no partiality (prosōpolēmpsia = face-receiving, partiality).
10: tou loipou, endynamousthe en kyriō kai en tō kratei tēs ischyos autou — "finally, be strengthened in the Lord and in the strength of his might" (endynamousthe: present passive imperative — keep being empowered, continue receiving strength): en kyriō (in the Lord) is again the sphere of divine strengthening. Kratei tēs ischyos (strength of his might): kratos (power, dominion) and ischys (strength, force) — the same doublet as 1:19 where God's power toward believers is described as to hyperballon megethos tēs dynameōs... kata tēn energeian tou kratous tēs ischyos autou. The armor section elaborates what this empowered-in-the-Lord state looks like concretely.
11: endysasthe tēn panoplian tou theou — "put on the full armor of God" (panoplia: the complete armor set of a heavy infantryman, covering all parts of the body — from pan + hopla): the term panoplia is rare and specifically military; Isaiah 59:17 LXX uses panoplia for the divine warrior's complete equipment. The genitive tou theou (of God) is both possessive (God's own armor) and source (armor that God provides): believers are equipped with the divine warrior's own equipment. Pros to dynasthai hymas stēnai pros tas methodias tou diabolou — "so that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil" (methodias: scheming, craftiness, strategic deception — used in 4:14 for human deception).
12: hoti ouk estin hēmin hē palē pros haima kai sarka alla pros tas archas, pros tas exousias, pros tous kosmokratoras tou skotous toutou, pros ta pneumatika tēs ponērias en tois epouraniois — "for our struggle is not against flesh and blood but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers of this present darkness, against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places" (palē: wrestling, grappling combat — the word for the ancient wrestling match): the four-term list of cosmic powers (archai, exousiai, kosmokratores, pneumatika tēs ponērias) recapitulates the powers language of 1:21 and Ephesians 3:10, now identified as the adversaries in the spiritual battle. Kosmokratoras tou skotous toutou (world-rulers of this darkness) — a rare compound; the cosmological dualism identifies a present-age power structure under the authority of darkness. En tois epouraniois (in the heavenly places) — the same sphere where Christ is exalted (Ephesians 1:20) and where the church is seated (Ephesians 2:6): the heavenly conflict is already partly decided by Christ's exaltation.
14: stēte oun perizōsamenoi tēn osphyn hymōn en alētheia kai endysamenoi ton thōraka tēs dikaiosynēs — "stand therefore, having fastened the belt of truth around your waist and having put on the breastplate of righteousness" (perizōsamenoi: perfect middle participle of perizōnnymi, to gird around): the belt/girdle of truth secures the other armor pieces (the zōnē held the sword and kept the breastplate in place). En alētheia (in truth): the prepared, secured stance of truthfulness that supports all other spiritual equipment. Thōraka tēs dikaiosynēs — "breastplate of righteousness": either (a) the imputed righteousness of Christ (the forensic gift that protects against condemnation); or (b) the practiced righteousness of upright life (the ethical armor); both senses likely operative, as Isaiah 59:17's divine warrior wears the righteousness that he both embodies and executes.
15: kai hypodēsamenoi tous podas en hetoimasia tou euangeliou tēs eirēnēs — "and having strapped on your feet the readiness of the gospel of peace" (hypodēsamenoi: having bound underneath, sandaled — the military sandal was the caliga, the hobnailed boot of the Roman legionary): hetoimasia (readiness, preparation, firm foundation) — the feet of the gospel-bearer are readied, stabilized by the message they carry. The gospel of peace is both the message proclaimed and the stabilizing force underfoot: the one who carries the peace-message stands firm because peace grounds them. Isaiah 52:7's feet of the good-news-bearer are the direct OT background.
16: en pasin analabontes ton thyreon tēs pisteōs — "in all [circumstances], having taken up the shield of faith" (thyreon: the large door-shaped rectangular shield of the Roman legionary, as opposed to the aspis, the smaller round shield): the thyreon was approximately 4 feet tall and 2.5 feet wide, designed to cover the whole body and to interlock with adjacent soldiers' shields in the testudo formation — a communal as well as individual defense. En hō dynēsesthe panta ta belē tou ponērou ta pepyrōmena sbesai — "with which you will be able to extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one" (pepyrōmena: having been set on fire — incendiary projectiles used in ancient siege warfare).
17: kai tēn perikephalaian tou sōtēriou dexasthe kai tēn machairan tou pneumatos, ho estin rhēma theou — "and receive the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God": dexasthe (receive, take) — the middle aorist imperative suggests an act of reception rather than self-equipping: the helmet is given, received. Perikephalaia tou sōtēriou (helmet of salvation): Isaiah 59:17 LXX reads perikephalaian sōtēriou — Paul uses the identical phrase with the article. Machairan tou pneumatos — "sword of the Spirit" (machaira: the short sword used in close combat, not the rhomphaia = long sword): ho estin rhēma theou — the appositive identifies the sword: rhēma (specific spoken word, utterance) rather than logos (the broader word/reason/message). The Spirit's sword is the specific, situationally appropriate divine word spoken in the moment.
18: dia pasēs proseuchēs kai deēseōs proseuchomenoi en panti kairō en pneumati — "praying at all times in the Spirit with every prayer and petition" (proseuchē: prayer generally; deēsis: specific supplication or petition for a need — the pairing covers both general communion and specific intercession): en panti kairō (at every opportune moment, at all times) — the kairological time of prayer: every occasion is an occasion for prayer. En pneumati (in the Spirit): the Spirit is the element and power in which prayer occurs; cf. Romans 8:26-27 where the Spirit intercedes within the believer's prayer. Eis auto agrypnountes en pasē proskarterēsei kai deēsei peri pantōn tōn hagiōn — "keeping alert with all perseverance, making supplication for all the saints": agrypneō (to be sleepless, stay awake, keep watch) — the prayer-watch language.
19: kai hyper emou, hina moi dothē logos en anoixei tou stomatos mou, en parrēsia gnōrisai to mystērion tou euangeliou — "and for me, that words may be given to me in opening my mouth boldly to make known the mystery of the gospel" (anoixei tou stomatos mou: in opening of my mouth — the idiom for beginning to speak publicly, Ezekiel 3:27; Ephesians 33:22; Acts 8:35): en parrēsia (with boldness, with freedom of speech) — the rhetorically confident, frank speech that fear might suppress; the same courage Paul will specify as the means of his ambassadorial task in v20. To mystērion tou euangeliou (the mystery of the gospel): the mysterion that has been the letter's major theme (Ephesians 1:9; Ephesians 3:3-6, 9; Ephesians 5:32) — the Jew-Gentile unity in Christ now applied to Paul's specific vocation to proclaim it.
20: hyper hou presbeuo en halysei, hina en autō parrēsiasōmai hōs dei me lalēsai — "for which I am an ambassador in chains, that I may declare it boldly as I ought to speak" (presbeuō: to be an ambassador, represent as an envoy — the official term for diplomatic representation of a superior): Paul uses the same term in 2 Corinthians 5:20 for the apostolic ambassadorship of reconciliation. The paradox of presbeuō en halysei (ambassador in chains/fetters) is deliberate: the highest diplomatic office combined with the prisoner's condition. Hōs dei me lalēsai (as it is necessary for me to speak) — the apostolic compulsion that overrides the circumstances of imprisonment.
24: hē charis meta pantōn tōn agapōntōn ton kyrion hēmōn Iēsoum Christon en aphtharsia — "grace be with all those who love our Lord Jesus Christ in incorruptibility/immortality" (en aphtharsia: in incorruptibility, with unfading love, with imperishability): the closing en aphtharsia is the most unusual element of any Pauline epistolary closing. It modifies either: (a) the agapōntōn (those who love with an undying love); or (b) the grace (that endures imperishably). Either reading places the eschatological dimension of the Christ-love at the forefront of the closing: the love of Christ that the community embodies is of the quality of the imperishable age, not the perishing present age.
7: met' eunoias douleuontes hōs tō kyriō kai ouk anthrōpois — "serving with good will as to the Lord and not to people" (eunoias: benevolence, good will — the generous inner disposition that makes service genuinely willing rather than grudging): the phrase completes and reinforces v6's reframing: service oriented to the Lord is characterized by eunoias, not by the calculated performance of the people-pleaser. The repetition of hōs tō kyriō kai ouk anthrōpois (as to the Lord and not to people) in successive verses is emphatic — the principle requires double declaration to be internalized.
8: eidotes hoti hekastos ean ti agathon poiēsē, touto komietai para kyriou, eite doulos eite eleutheros — "knowing that each one, if he does anything good, will receive this back from the Lord, whether slave or free" (eidotes: knowing — participial grounding; behavioral transformation flows from knowledge of eschatological reality): komizō (to receive, carry away) has a commercial register suggesting receiving what is due — the eschatological recompense is cast as a settled accounting. Eite doulos eite eleutheros (whether slave or free) — the social leveling is the most radical implication of the Christ-slavery framework: the Lord's assessment operates independently of the earthly status distinction, registering every good act regardless of whether the actor has social standing. This is the ground on which the slave's faithful service has eternal weight equal to the free person's.
13: dia touto analabete tēn panoplian tou theou — "therefore take up the full armor of God" (analabete: aorist active imperative — a decisive act of reception): the dia touto (therefore, because of this) grounds the imperative in the nature of the opponents named in v12: because the adversaries are cosmic powers, not human antagonists, only God's own armor suffices. hina dynēthēte antistēnai en tē hēmera tē ponēra — "that you may be able to withstand in the evil day" (antistēnai: to stand against, resist — aorist active infinitive): hē hēmera hē ponēra (the evil day) may refer to (a) the final eschatological crisis; (b) any acute day of spiritual testing; both senses are supported by the letter's already-and-not-yet eschatology. hapanta katergazamenoi stēnai — "having done/worked through everything, to stand firm": katergazomai (to accomplish through sustained effort, work to completion) followed by stēnai — the full armor enables both active engagement with the enemy and the final firm stance after the engagement.
21: Tychikos ho agapētos adelphos kai pistos diakonos en kyriō gnōrisei hymin panta ta kat' eme — "Tychicus, the dear brother and faithful servant in the Lord, will make known to you all things about me" (Tychikos: attested in Acts 20:4 as an Asian co-traveler; the letter-carrier in Colossians 4:7-8, 2 Timothy 4:12, Titus 3:12): Tychicus functions as the authorized interpreter of the letter, carrying not just the document but information about Paul's situation that the letter itself does not supply. Agapētos adelphos kai pistos diakonos en kyriō (dear brother and faithful servant in the Lord) — the double honorific establishes Tychicus's trustworthiness as a tradent; the community can receive his oral report as reliably as the letter. This reliance on a letter-carrier for supplemental information was standard Hellenistic epistolary practice.
22: hon epempsa pros hymas eis auto touto, hina gnōte ta peri hēmōn kai parakalesē tas kardias hymōn — "whom I have sent to you for this very purpose, that you may know the things about us and that he may encourage your hearts" (eis auto touto: for this very thing, deliberately and specifically — the commission is purposive): parakalesē tas kardias hymōn (that he may encourage/exhort your hearts) — parakaleō encompasses both comfort and exhortation; kardias (hearts, the center of inner life and decision) is the target of Tychicus's ministry. The community's hearts may be troubled by Paul's imprisonment (3:13 already addressed this: "do not lose heart"); Tychicus's visit and report are the pastoral means of stabilizing that inner distress. The sending of a trusted envoy to carry and explain a letter is the normal practice of Pauline mission correspondence.
23: eirēnē tois adelphois kai agapē meta pisteōs apo theou patros kai kyriou Iēsou Christou — "peace to the brothers and sisters, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ": the closing blessing mirrors the letter's opening (charis hymin kai eirēnē apo theou patros hēmōn kai kyriou Iēsou Christou, Ephesians 1:2), completing the epistolary frame. Agapē meta pisteōs (love with faith) — an unusual pairing: the two are joined as inseparable; the love that characterizes the community is a love that flows from and is sustained by faith. Apo theou patros kai kyriou Iēsou Christou — the double divine source is the same high Christological formula as Ephesians 1:2; the letter that began with Christ and God as co-sources of grace and peace closes with them as co-sources of peace and love.
